blurb: Easy peppery-edible annual — round lily-pad leaves and spurred jewel-toned flowers that trail or climb, blooming nonstop till frost.
sun: Full sun (part shade in extreme heat)
water: Moderate; lean soil yields more flowers
seasons: Round lily-pad leaves & spurred orange/red/yellow flowers, summer to frost
toxic: Edible — peppery leaves, flowers & green seed pods are all edible
origin: South America — Andes of Peru and Colombia
habitat: Moist disturbed slopes and stream margins in Andean foothills

care.hardiness: A frost-tender warm-season annual (grown as an annual everywhere; USDA 2a–11b as a summer crop) — it is killed by the first hard frost, so on the Front Range treat it as a one-season plant sown after the mid-May last-frost date and pulled when fall frost blackens it.[3][1]
care.planting: Direct-sow in the ground after the danger of frost has passed — early to mid-May on the Front Range — because nasturtium resents transplanting and does poorly when its roots are disturbed.[2][1] In containers it grows beautifully (the trailing types spill from pots and window boxes); sow the seed straight into the pot at the same May timing in any well-drained potting mix, and remember pots dry out and overheat faster than ground beds in Boulder's intense summer sun, so site them where they get a little afternoon relief in a heat wave.[3][1]
care.sun: Full sun for the heaviest bloom; in the Front Range's intense high-altitude summer sun a little afternoon shade prevents heat stress and keeps the foliage from turning overly pungent. Too much shade reduces flowering.[3][2]
care.soil: Poor to average, well-drained soil — lean soil is genuinely better here, because rich or fertilized ground pushes lush leaves at the expense of flowers. Boulder's alkaline clay is fine as long as it drains; it tolerates drought and neglect.[3][2]
care.water: Moderate, even moisture. Keep plants consistently watered through the dry Front Range summer — adequate moisture moderates the leaf and flower spiciness, while heat stress and drought make the foliage overly pungent. Don't overwater, and let the surface dry between soakings.[1][3]
care.spacing: Space plants about 8–12 in apart; bush types stay around 1 ft, while trailing/climbing types run 6–10 ft and want a fence, trellis, or edge to spill over.[1][3]
care.sow: Sow seed directly where it is to grow, about 0.5–1 in deep, once soil has warmed after frost (early–mid May on the Front Range). The large seeds germinate readily; soaking them overnight speeds sprouting, and keeping the seedbed moist until emergence helps.[2][1]
care.bloom: Blooms through the summer, roughly July into fall on the Front Range, flowering nonstop until the first hard frost cuts it down.[1][2]
care.feeding: Do not feed. Nasturtium flowers best in lean soil — fertilizing increases foliage at the expense of flowers, so skip the fertilizer entirely for the best bloom.[3][2]
care.maintenance: Very low-maintenance. Deadheading spent flowers and picking leaves and blooms for the kitchen keeps plants tidy and productive; pull and compost the whole plant after frost.[2][1]
care.selfsow: It self-sows lightly — a few volunteers often come up the following spring from dropped seed. The volunteers are easy to pull or move while small; it is not aggressive or weedy on the Front Range.[3]
care.troubles: Aphids are the signature pest and the reason nasturtium is grown as a classic trap crop — it lures aphids (and whiteflies, mealybugs) away from vegetables nearby; hose off or tolerate the colonies. Otherwise it is largely trouble-free and deer-resistant.[3][1]
care.harvest: Pick young leaves and fully open flowers for the kitchen throughout the season — younger leaves are the most tender and least bitter, and flowers are mildest. Gather in the morning and use fresh (they don't dry well); store rinsed leaves and flowers in a plastic bag in the refrigerator. Harvest the immature green seed pods while still soft and bright green to pickle as "poor man's capers."[1][2]
care.propagation: By seed only — collect the dried mature seeds in fall and sow them the next spring after frost, or let the plant self-sow. The seeds are large and easy to handle; nasturtium does not propagate by cuttings or division.[3][2]
care.companions: A textbook companion/trap crop: interplant it among squash, cucumbers, beans, brassicas, and tomatoes, where it draws aphids and other pests away from the food crops while its flowers feed bees and hummingbirds.[3][1]

edible.summary: A genuinely edible flower: the leaves, flowers, flower buds, and immature green seed pods are all edible raw, with a peppery, watercress-like bite.[1][2]
edible.parts: The young leaves, the open flowers, the flower buds, and the immature (green, still-soft) seed pods are all edible. Younger leaves are the most tender and flavorful; older leaves turn bitter, and the flowers are milder than the leaves.[1][2][3]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves and flowers fresh — toss them into green and potato salads, egg and seafood dishes, and herb vinegars, or use the bright flowers as an edible garnish (they don't dry well, so use fresh).[1][2] The flavor is peppery and cress-like, from the same mustard-oil (glucosinolate) compounds as garden cress.[3] Pickle the immature green seed pods in vinegar as a caper substitute — the classic "poor man's capers."[2][1]
edible.caution: Eat only flowers and foliage you know are nasturtium and that were grown without pesticides or other chemicals; rinse thoroughly before eating.[1] Introduce edible flowers slowly into your diet and eat in moderation — the peppery glucosinolate compounds are pungent and can be irritating in quantity, and anyone with kidney issues should be cautious with the mustard-oil content.[1][4] Note that nasturtium is listed as toxic to cats, dogs, and horses, so keep pets from grazing on it even though it is fine for people.[3]
